GLOUCESTER: Now is the winter of our discontent

2.play: Made glorious summer by this sun of York;

3.play: And all the clouds that lour'd upon our house

4.play: In the deep bosom of the ocean buried.

5.play: Now are our brows bound with victorious wreaths;

6.play: Our bruised arms hung up for monuments;

7.play: Our stern alarums changed to merry meetings,

8.play: Our dreadful marches to delightful measures.

9.play: Grim-visaged war hath smooth'd his wrinkled front;

10.play: And now, instead of mounting barbed steeds

11.play: To fright the souls of fearful adversaries,

12.play: He capers nimbly in a lady's chamber

13.play: To the lascivious pleasing of a lute.

14.play: But I, that am not shaped for sportive tricks,

15.play: Nor made to court an amorous looking-glass;

16.play: I, that am rudely stamp'd, and want love's majesty

17.play: To strut before a wanton ambling nymph;

18.play: I, that am curtail'd of this fair proportion,

19.play: Cheated of feature by dissembling nature,

20.play: Deformed, unfinish'd, sent before my time

21.play: Into this breathing world, scarce half made up,

22.play: And that so lamely and unfashionable

23.play: That dogs bark at me as I halt by them;

24.play: Why, I, in this weak piping time of peace,

25.play: Have no delight to pass away the time,

26.play: Unless to spy my shadow in the sun

27.play: And descant on mine own deformity:

28.play: And therefore, since I cannot prove a lover,

29.play: To entertain these fair well-spoken days,

30.play: I am determined to prove a villain

31.play: And hate the idle pleasures of these days.

32.play: Plots have I laid, inductions dangerous,

33.play: By drunken prophecies, libels and dreams,

34.play: To set my brother Clarence and the king

35.play: In deadly hate the one against the other:

36.play: And if King Edward be as true and just

37.play: As I am subtle, false and treacherous,

38.play: This day should Clarence closely be mew'd up,

39.play: About a prophecy, which says that 'G'

40.play: Of Edward's heirs the murderer shall be.

41.play: Dive, thoughts, down to my soul: here

43.play: Brother, good day; what means this armed guard

46.play: Tendering my person's safety, hath appointed

47.play: This conduct to convey me to the Tower.

49.play: CLARENCE: Because my name is George.

50.play: GLOUCESTER: Alack, my lord, that fault is none of yours;

51.play: He should, for that, commit your godfathers:

52.play: O, belike his majesty hath some intent

53.play: That you shall be new-christen'd in the Tower.

54.play: But what's the matter, Clarence? may I know?

55.play: CLARENCE: Yea, Richard, when I know; for I protest

56.play: As yet I do not: but, as I can learn,

57.play: He hearkens after prophecies and dreams;

58.play: And from the cross-row plucks the letter G.

59.play: And says a wizard told him that by G

61.play: And, for my name of George begins with G,

62.play: It follows in his thought that I am he.

63.play: These, as I learn, and such like toys as these

64.play: Have moved his highness to commit me now.

65.play: GLOUCESTER: Why, this it is, when men are ruled by women:

66.play: 'Tis not the king that sends you to the Tower:

67.play: My Lady Grey his wife, Clarence, 'tis she

68.play: That tempers him to this extremity.

69.play: Was it not she and that good man of worship,

71.play: That made him send Lord Hastings to the Tower,

72.play: From whence this present day he is deliver'd?

73.play: We are not safe, Clarence; we are not safe.

74.play: CLARENCE: By heaven, I think there's no man is secure

75.play: But the queen's kindred and night-walking heralds

76.play: That trudge betwixt the king and Mistress Shore.

77.play: Heard ye not what an humble suppliant

78.play: Lord hastings was to her for his delivery?

79.play: GLOUCESTER: Humbly complaining to her deity

80.play: Got my lord chamberlain his liberty.

81.play: I'll tell you what; I think it is our way,

82.play: If we will keep in favour with the king,

83.play: To be her men and wear her livery:

84.play: The jealous o'erworn widow and herself,

85.play: Since that our brother dubb'd them gentlewomen.

86.play: Are mighty gossips in this monarchy.

87.play: BRAKENBURY: I beseech your graces both to pardon me;

88.play: His majesty hath straitly given in charge

89.play: That no man shall have private conference,

90.play: Of what degree soever, with his brother.

91.play: GLOUCESTER: Even so; an't please your worship, Brakenbury,

92.play: You may partake of any thing we say:

93.play: We speak no treason, man: we say the king

94.play: Is wise and virtuous, and his noble queen

95.play: Well struck in years, fair, and not jealous;

96.play: We say that Shore's wife hath a pretty foot,

97.play: A cherry lip, a bonny eye, a passing pleasing tongue;

98.play: And that the queen's kindred are made gentle-folks:

99.play: How say you sir? Can you deny all this?

100.play: BRAKENBURY: With this, my lord, myself have nought to do.

101.play: GLOUCESTER: Naught to do with mistress Shore! I tell thee, fellow,

102.play: He that doth naught with her, excepting one,

103.play: Were best he do it secretly, alone.

105.play: GLOUCESTER: Her husband, knave: wouldst thou betray me?

106.play: BRAKENBURY: I beseech your grace to pardon me, and withal

107.play: Forbear your conference with the noble duke.

108.play: CLARENCE: We know thy charge, Brakenbury, and will obey.

109.play: GLOUCESTER: We are the queen's abjects, and must obey.

110.play: Brother, farewell: I will unto the king;

111.play: And whatsoever you will employ me in,

112.play: Were it to call King Edward's widow sister,

113.play: I will perform it to enfranchise you.

114.play: Meantime, this deep disgrace in brotherhood

115.play: Touches me deeper than you can imagine.

116.play: CLARENCE: I know it pleaseth neither of us well.

117.play: GLOUCESTER: Well, your imprisonment shall not be long;

120.play: GLOUCESTER: Go, tread the path that thou shalt ne'er return.

121.play: Simple, plain Clarence! I do love thee so,

122.play: That I will shortly send thy soul to heaven,

123.play: If heaven will take the present at our hands.

124.play: But who comes here? the new-deliver'd Hastings?

125.play: HASTINGS: Good time of day unto my gracious lord!

126.play: GLOUCESTER: As much unto my good lord chamberlain!

127.play: Well are you welcome to the open air.

128.play: How hath your lordship brook'd imprisonment?

129.play: HASTINGS: With patience, noble lord, as prisoners must:

130.play: But I shall live, my lord, to give them thanks

131.play: That were the cause of my imprisonment.

132.play: GLOUCESTER: No doubt, no doubt; and so shall Clarence too;

133.play: For they that were your enemies are his,

134.play: And have prevail'd as much on him as you.

135.play: HASTINGS: More pity that the eagle should be mew'd,

136.play: While kites and buzzards prey at liberty.

138.play: HASTINGS: No news so bad abroad as this at home;

139.play: The King is sickly, weak and melancholy,

140.play: And his physicians fear him mightily.

141.play: GLOUCESTER: Now, by Saint Paul, this news is bad indeed.

142.play: O, he hath kept an evil diet long,

143.play: And overmuch consumed his royal person:

144.play: 'Tis very grievous to be thought upon.

145.play: What, is he in his bed?

147.play: GLOUCESTER: Go you before, and I will follow you.

148.play: He cannot live, I hope; and must not die

149.play: Till George be pack'd with post-horse up to heaven.

150.play: I'll in, to urge his hatred more to Clarence,

151.play: With lies well steel'd with weighty arguments;

152.play: And, if I fall not in my deep intent,

153.play: Clarence hath not another day to live:

154.play: Which done, God take King Edward to his mercy,

155.play: And leave the world for me to bustle in!

156.play: For then I'll marry Warwick's youngest daughter.

157.play: What though I kill'd her husband and her father?

158.play: The readiest way to make the wench amends

159.play: Is to become her husband and her father:

160.play: The which will I; not all so much for love

161.play: As for another secret close intent,

162.play: By marrying her which I must reach unto.

163.play: But yet I run before my horse to market:

164.play: Clarence still breathes; Edward still lives and reigns:

165.play: When they are gone, then must I count my gains.

1.play: LADY ANNE: Set down, set down your honourable load,

2.play: If honour may be shrouded in a hearse,

3.play: Whilst I awhile obsequiously lament

4.play: The untimely fall of virtuous Lancaster.

5.play: Poor key-cold figure of a holy king!

6.play: Pale ashes of the house of Lancaster!

7.play: Thou bloodless remnant of that royal blood!

8.play: Be it lawful that I invocate thy ghost,

9.play: To hear the lamentations of Poor Anne,

10.play: Wife to thy Edward, to thy slaughter'd son,

11.play: Stabb'd by the selfsame hand that made these wounds!

12.play: Lo, in these windows that let forth thy life,

13.play: I pour the helpless balm of my poor eyes.

14.play: Cursed be the hand that made these fatal holes!

15.play: Cursed be the heart that had the heart to do it!

16.play: Cursed the blood that let this blood from hence!

17.play: More direful hap betide that hated wretch,

18.play: That makes us wretched by the death of thee,

19.play: Than I can wish to adders, spiders, toads,

20.play: Or any creeping venom'd thing that lives!

21.play: If ever he have child, abortive be it,

22.play: Prodigious, and untimely brought to light,

23.play: Whose ugly and unnatural aspect

24.play: May fright the hopeful mother at the view;

25.play: And that be heir to his unhappiness!

26.play: If ever he have wife, let her he made

27.play: A miserable by the death of him

28.play: As I am made by my poor lord and thee!

29.play: Come, now towards Chertsey with your holy load,

30.play: Taken from Paul's to be interred there;

31.play: And still, as you are weary of the weight,

32.play: Rest you, whiles I lament King Henry's corse.

33.play: GLOUCESTER: Stay, you that bear the corse, and set it down.

34.play: LADY ANNE: What black magician conjures up this fiend,

36.play: Villains, set down the corse; or, by Saint Paul,

37.play: I'll make a corse of him that disobeys.

38.play: Gentleman: My lord, stand back, and let the coffin pass.

39.play: Unmanner'd dog! stand thou, when I command:

40.play: Advance thy halbert higher than my breast,

41.play: Or, by Saint Paul, I'll strike thee to my foot,

42.play: And spurn upon thee, beggar, for thy boldness.

43.play: LADY ANNE: What, do you tremble? are you all afraid?

44.play: Alas, I blame you not; for you are mortal,

45.play: And mortal eyes cannot endure the devil.

46.play: Avaunt, thou dreadful minister of hell!

47.play: Thou hadst but power over his mortal body,

48.play: His soul thou canst not have; therefore be gone.

49.play: GLOUCESTER: Sweet saint, for charity, be not so curst.

50.play: LADY ANNE: Foul devil, for God's sake, hence, and trouble us not;

51.play: For thou hast made the happy earth thy hell,

52.play: Fill'd it with cursing cries and deep exclaims.

53.play: If thou delight to view thy heinous deeds,

54.play: Behold this pattern of thy butcheries.

55.play: O, gentlemen, see, see! dead Henry's wounds

56.play: Open their congeal'd mouths and bleed afresh!

57.play: Blush, Blush, thou lump of foul deformity;

58.play: For 'tis thy presence that exhales this blood

59.play: From cold and empty veins, where no blood dwells;

62.play: O God, which this blood madest, revenge his death!

63.play: O earth, which this blood drink'st revenge his death!

64.play: Either heaven with lightning strike the

66.play: Or earth, gape open wide and eat him quick,

67.play: As thou dost swallow up this good king's blood

68.play: Which his hell-govern'd arm hath butchered!

69.play: Lady, you know no rules of charity,

70.play: Which renders good for bad, blessings for curses.

71.play: LADY ANNE: Villain, thou know'st no law of God nor man:

72.play: No beast so fierce but knows some touch of pity.

73.play: But I know none, and therefore am no beast.

74.play: LADY ANNE: O wonderful, when devils tell the truth!

75.play: More wonderful, when angels are so angry.

76.play: Vouchsafe, divine perfection of a woman,

77.play: Of these supposed-evils, to give me leave,

78.play: By circumstance, but to acquit myself.

79.play: LADY ANNE: Vouchsafe, defused infection of a man,

80.play: For these known evils, but to give me leave,

81.play: By circumstance, to curse thy cursed self.

82.play: Fairer than tongue can name thee, let me have

83.play: Some patient leisure to excuse myself.

84.play: LADY ANNE: Fouler than heart can think thee, thou canst make

85.play: No excuse current, but to hang thyself.

86.play: By such despair, I should accuse myself.

87.play: LADY ANNE: And, by despairing, shouldst thou stand excused;

88.play: For doing worthy vengeance on thyself,

89.play: Which didst unworthy slaughter upon others.

90.play: Say that I slew them not?

91.play: LADY ANNE: Why, then they are not dead:

92.play: But dead they are, and devilish slave, by thee.

93.play: I did not kill your husband.

94.play: LADY ANNE: Why, then he is alive.

95.play: Nay, he is dead; and slain by Edward's hand.

96.play: LADY ANNE: In thy foul throat thou liest: Queen Margaret saw

97.play: Thy murderous falchion smoking in his blood;

98.play: The which thou once didst bend against her breast,

99.play: But that thy brothers beat aside the point.

100.play: I was provoked by her slanderous tongue,

101.play: which laid their guilt upon my guiltless shoulders.

102.play: LADY ANNE: Thou wast provoked by thy bloody mind.

103.play: Which never dreamt on aught but butcheries:

104.play: Didst thou not kill this king?

106.play: LADY ANNE: Dost grant me, hedgehog? then, God grant me too

107.play: Thou mayst be damned for that wicked deed!

108.play: O, he was gentle, mild, and virtuous!

109.play: The fitter for the King of heaven, that hath him.

110.play: LADY ANNE: He is in heaven, where thou shalt never come.

111.play: Let him thank me, that holp to send him thither;

112.play: For he was fitter for that place than earth.

113.play: LADY ANNE: And thou unfit for any place but hell.

114.play: Yes, one place else, if you will hear me name it.

117.play: LADY ANNE: Ill rest betide the chamber where thou liest!

118.play: So will it, madam till I lie with you.

120.play: I know so. But, gentle Lady Anne,

121.play: To leave this keen encounter of our wits,

122.play: And fall somewhat into a slower method,

123.play: Is not the causer of the timeless deaths

124.play: Of these Plantagenets, Henry and Edward,

126.play: LADY ANNE: Thou art the cause, and most accursed effect.

127.play: Your beauty was the cause of that effect;

128.play: Your beauty: which did haunt me in my sleep

129.play: To undertake the death of all the world,

130.play: So I might live one hour in your sweet bosom.

131.play: LADY ANNE: If I thought that, I tell thee, homicide,

132.play: These nails should rend that beauty from my cheeks.

133.play: These eyes could never endure sweet beauty's wreck;

134.play: You should not blemish it, if I stood by:

135.play: As all the world is cheered by the sun,

136.play: So I by that; it is my day, my life.

137.play: LADY ANNE: Black night o'ershade thy day, and death thy life!

138.play: Curse not thyself, fair creature thou art both.

139.play: LADY ANNE: I would I were, to be revenged on thee.

140.play: It is a quarrel most unnatural,

141.play: To be revenged on him that loveth you.

142.play: LADY ANNE: It is a quarrel just and reasonable,

143.play: To be revenged on him that slew my husband.

144.play: He that bereft thee, lady, of thy husband,

145.play: Did it to help thee to a better husband.

146.play: LADY ANNE: His better doth not breathe upon the earth.

147.play: He lives that loves thee better than he could.

150.play: LADY ANNE: Why, that was he.

151.play: The selfsame name, but one of better nature.

154.play: Why dost thou spit at me?

155.play: LADY ANNE: Would it were mortal poison, for thy sake!

156.play: Never came poison from so sweet a place.

157.play: LADY ANNE: Never hung poison on a fouler toad.

158.play: Out of my sight! thou dost infect my eyes.

159.play: Thine eyes, sweet lady, have infected mine.

160.play: LADY ANNE: Would they were basilisks, to strike thee dead!

161.play: I would they were, that I might die at once;

162.play: For now they kill me with a living death.

163.play: Those eyes of thine from mine have drawn salt tears,

164.play: Shamed their aspect with store of childish drops:

165.play: These eyes that never shed remorseful tear,

166.play: No, when my father York and Edward wept,

167.play: To hear the piteous moan that Rutland made

168.play: When black-faced Clifford shook his sword at him;

169.play: Nor when thy warlike father, like a child,

170.play: Told the sad story of my father's death,

171.play: And twenty times made pause to sob and weep,

172.play: That all the standers-by had wet their cheeks

173.play: Like trees bedash'd with rain: in that sad time

174.play: My manly eyes did scorn an humble tear;

175.play: And what these sorrows could not thence exhale,

176.play: Thy beauty hath, and made them blind with weeping.

177.play: I never sued to friend nor enemy;

178.play: My tongue could never learn sweet smoothing word;

179.play: But now thy beauty is proposed my fee,

180.play: My proud heart sues, and prompts my tongue to speak.

181.play: Teach not thy lips such scorn, for they were made

182.play: For kissing, lady, not for such contempt.

183.play: If thy revengeful heart cannot forgive,

184.play: Lo, here I lend thee this sharp-pointed sword;

185.play: Which if thou please to hide in this true bosom.

187.play: I lay it naked to the deadly stroke,

1.play: RIVERS: Have patience, madam: there's no doubt his majesty

2.play: Will soon recover his accustom'd health.

3.play: GREY: In that you brook it in, it makes him worse:

4.play: Therefore, for God's sake, entertain good comfort,

5.play: And cheer his grace with quick and merry words.

6.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: If he were dead, what would betide of me?

7.play: RIVERS: No other harm but loss of such a lord.

8.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: The loss of such a lord includes all harm.

10.play: To be your comforter when he is gone.

12.play: Is put unto the trust of Richard Gloucester,

13.play: A man that loves not me, nor none of you.

154.play: Second Murderer: A man, as you are.

155.play: But not, as I am, royal.

156.play: Nor you, as we are, loyal.

157.play: Thy voice is thunder, but thy looks are humble.

158.play: My voice is now the king's, my looks mine own.

159.play: How darkly and how deadly dost thou speak!

160.play: Your eyes do menace me: why look you pale?

161.play: Who sent you hither? Wherefore do you come?

165.play: You scarcely have the hearts to tell me so,

166.play: And therefore cannot have the hearts to do it.

167.play: Wherein, my friends, have I offended you?

168.play: First Murderer: Offended us you have not, but the king.

169.play: I shall be reconciled to him again.

170.play: Second Murderer: Never, my lord; therefore prepare to die.

171.play: Are you call'd forth from out a world of men

172.play: To slay the innocent? What is my offence?

173.play: Where are the evidence that do accuse me?

174.play: What lawful quest have given their verdict up

175.play: Unto the frowning judge? or who pronounced

176.play: The bitter sentence of poor Clarence' death?

177.play: Before I be convict by course of law,

178.play: To threaten me with death is most unlawful.

179.play: I charge you, as you hope to have redemption

180.play: By Christ's dear blood shed for our grievous sins,

181.play: That you depart and lay no hands on me

182.play: The deed you undertake is damnable.

183.play: First Murderer: What we will do, we do upon command.

184.play: Second Murderer: And he that hath commanded is the king.

185.play: Erroneous vassal! the great King of kings

186.play: Hath in the tables of his law commanded

187.play: That thou shalt do no murder: and wilt thou, then,

188.play: Spurn at his edict and fulfil a man's?

189.play: Take heed; for he holds vengeance in his hands,

190.play: To hurl upon their heads that break his law.

191.play: And that same vengeance doth he hurl on thee,

192.play: For false forswearing and for murder too:

193.play: Thou didst receive the holy sacrament,

194.play: To fight in quarrel of the house of Lancaster.

195.play: First Murderer: And, like a traitor to the name of God,

196.play: Didst break that vow; and with thy treacherous blade

197.play: Unrip'dst the bowels of thy sovereign's son.

198.play: Second Murderer: Whom thou wert sworn to cherish and defend.

199.play: First Murderer: How canst thou urge God's dreadful law to us,

200.play: When thou hast broke it in so dear degree?

201.play: Alas! for whose sake did I that ill deed?

202.play: For Edward, for my brother, for his sake: Why, sirs,

203.play: He sends ye not to murder me for this

204.play: For in this sin he is as deep as I.

205.play: If God will be revenged for this deed.

206.play: O, know you yet, he doth it publicly,

207.play: Take not the quarrel from his powerful arm;

208.play: He needs no indirect nor lawless course

209.play: To cut off those that have offended him.

210.play: Who made thee, then, a bloody minister,

212.play: That princely novice, was struck dead by thee?

213.play: My brother's love, the devil, and my rage.

214.play: Thy brother's love, our duty, and thy fault,

215.play: Provoke us hither now to slaughter thee.

216.play: Oh, if you love my brother, hate not me;

217.play: I am his brother, and I love him well.

218.play: If you be hired for meed, go back again,

219.play: And I will send you to my brother Gloucester,

220.play: Who shall reward you better for my life

221.play: Than Edward will for tidings of my death.

222.play: Second Murderer: You are deceived, your brother Gloucester hates you.

223.play: O, no, he loves me, and he holds me dear:

224.play: Go you to him from me.

226.play: Tell him, when that our princely father York

227.play: Bless'd his three sons with his victorious arm,

228.play: And charged us from his soul to love each other,

229.play: He little thought of this divided friendship:

230.play: Bid Gloucester think of this, and he will weep.

231.play: First Murderer: Ay, millstones; as be lesson'd us to weep.

232.play: O, do not slander him, for he is kind.

234.play: As snow in harvest. Thou deceivest thyself:

235.play: 'Tis he that sent us hither now to slaughter thee.

236.play: It cannot be; for when I parted with him,

237.play: He hugg'd me in his arms, and swore, with sobs,

238.play: That he would labour my delivery.

239.play: Second Murderer: Why, so he doth, now he delivers thee

240.play: From this world's thraldom to the joys of heaven.

241.play: First Murderer: Make peace with God, for you must die, my lord.

242.play: Hast thou that holy feeling in thy soul,

243.play: To counsel me to make my peace with God,

244.play: And art thou yet to thy own soul so blind,

245.play: That thou wilt war with God by murdering me?

246.play: Ah, sirs, consider, he that set you on

247.play: To do this deed will hate you for the deed.

248.play: Second Murderer: What shall we do?

250.play: First Murderer: Relent! 'tis cowardly and womanish.

251.play: Not to relent is beastly, savage, devilish.

252.play: Which of you, if you were a prince's son,

253.play: Being pent from liberty, as I am now,

254.play: if two such murderers as yourselves came to you,

256.play: My friend, I spy some pity in thy looks:

257.play: O, if thine eye be not a flatterer,

258.play: Come thou on my side, and entreat for me,

259.play: As you would beg, were you in my distress

260.play: A begging prince what beggar pities not?

261.play: Second Murderer: Look behind you, my lord.

262.play: First Murderer: Take that, and that: if all this will not do,

263.play: I'll drown you in the malmsey-butt within.

264.play: Second Murderer: A bloody deed, and desperately dispatch'd!

265.play: How fain, like Pilate, would I wash my hands

266.play: Of this most grievous guilty murder done!

267.play: First Murderer: How now! what mean'st thou, that thou help'st me not?

268.play: By heavens, the duke shall know how slack thou art!

269.play: Second Murderer: I would he knew that I had saved his brother!

270.play: Take thou the fee, and tell him what I say;

271.play: For I repent me that the duke is slain.

1.play: KING EDWARD IV: Why, so: now have I done a good day's work:

2.play: You peers, continue this united league:

3.play: I every day expect an embassage

4.play: From my Redeemer to redeem me hence;

5.play: And now in peace my soul shall part to heaven,

6.play: Since I have set my friends at peace on earth.

7.play: Rivers and Hastings, take each other's hand;

8.play: Dissemble not your hatred, swear your love.

9.play: RIVERS: By heaven, my heart is purged from grudging hate:

10.play: And with my hand I seal my true heart's love.

11.play: HASTINGS: So thrive I, as I truly swear the like!

12.play: KING EDWARD IV: Take heed you dally not before your king;

13.play: Lest he that is the supreme King of kings

14.play: Confound your hidden falsehood, and award

15.play: Either of you to be the other's end.

16.play: So prosper I, as I swear perfect love!

17.play: RIVERS: And I, as I love Hastings with my heart!

18.play: KING EDWARD IV: Madam, yourself are not exempt in this,

19.play: Nor your son Dorset, Buckingham, nor you;

20.play: You have been factious one against the other,

21.play: Wife, love Lord Hastings, let him kiss your hand;

22.play: And what you do, do it unfeignedly.

23.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Here, Hastings; I will never more remember

24.play: Our former hatred, so thrive I and mine!

25.play: KING EDWARD IV: Dorset, embrace him; Hastings, love lord marquess.

26.play: DORSET: This interchange of love, I here protest,

27.play: Upon my part shall be unviolable.

28.play: HASTINGS: And so swear I, my lord

29.play: KING EDWARD IV: Now, princely Buckingham, seal thou this league

30.play: With thy embracements to my wife's allies,

31.play: And make me happy in your unity.

32.play: BUCKINGHAM: Whenever Buckingham doth turn his hate

35.play: Doth cherish you and yours, God punish me

36.play: With hate in those where I expect most love!

37.play: When I have most need to employ a friend,

38.play: And most assured that he is a friend

39.play: Deep, hollow, treacherous, and full of guile,

40.play: Be he unto me! this do I beg of God,

41.play: When I am cold in zeal to yours.

42.play: KING EDWARD IV: A pleasing cordial, princely Buckingham,

43.play: is this thy vow unto my sickly heart.

44.play: There wanteth now our brother Gloucester here,

45.play: To make the perfect period of this peace.

46.play: And, in good time, here comes the noble duke.

47.play: GLOUCESTER: Good morrow to my sovereign king and queen:

48.play: And, princely peers, a happy time of day!

49.play: KING EDWARD IV: Happy, indeed, as we have spent the day.

50.play: Brother, we done deeds of charity;

51.play: Made peace enmity, fair love of hate,

53.play: A blessed labour, my most sovereign liege:

54.play: Amongst this princely heap, if any here,

55.play: By false intelligence, or wrong surmise,

57.play: If I unwittingly, or in my rage,

58.play: Have aught committed that is hardly borne

59.play: By any in this presence, I desire

60.play: To reconcile me to his friendly peace:

61.play: 'Tis death to me to be at enmity;

62.play: I hate it, and desire all good men's love.

63.play: First, madam, I entreat true peace of you,

64.play: Which I will purchase with my duteous service;

65.play: Of you, my noble cousin Buckingham,

66.play: If ever any grudge were lodged between us;

67.play: Of you, Lord Rivers, and, Lord Grey, of you;

68.play: That without desert have frown'd on me;

69.play: Dukes, earls, lords, gentlemen; indeed, of all.

70.play: I do not know that Englishman alive

71.play: With whom my soul is any jot at odds

72.play: More than the infant that is born to-night

73.play: I thank my God for my humility.

74.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: A holy day shall this be kept hereafter:

75.play: I would to God all strifes were well compounded.

76.play: My sovereign liege, I do beseech your majesty

77.play: To take our brother Clarence to your grace.

78.play: Why, madam, have I offer'd love for this

79.play: To be so bouted in this royal presence?

80.play: Who knows not that the noble duke is dead?

81.play: You do him injury to scorn his corse.

82.play: RIVERS: Who knows not he is dead! who knows he is?

83.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: All seeing heaven, what a world is this!

84.play: BUCKINGHAM: Look I so pale, Lord Dorset, as the rest?

85.play: DORSET: Ay, my good lord; and no one in this presence

86.play: But his red colour hath forsook his cheeks.

87.play: KING EDWARD IV: Is Clarence dead? the order was reversed.

88.play: GLOUCESTER: But he, poor soul, by your first order died,

89.play: And that a winged Mercury did bear:

90.play: Some tardy cripple bore the countermand,

91.play: That came too lag to see him buried.

92.play: God grant that some, less noble and less loyal,

93.play: Nearer in bloody thoughts, but not in blood,

94.play: Deserve not worse than wretched Clarence did,

95.play: And yet go current from suspicion!

96.play: DERBY: A boon, my sovereign, for my service done!

97.play: KING EDWARD IV: I pray thee, peace: my soul is full of sorrow.

98.play: I will not rise, unless your highness grant.

99.play: KING EDWARD IV: Then speak at once what is it thou demand'st.

100.play: The forfeit, sovereign, of my servant's life;

101.play: Who slew to-day a righteous gentleman

102.play: Lately attendant on the Duke of Norfolk.

103.play: KING EDWARD IV: Have a tongue to doom my brother's death,

104.play: And shall the same give pardon to a slave?

105.play: My brother slew no man; his fault was thought,

106.play: And yet his punishment was cruel death.

107.play: Who sued to me for him? who, in my rage,

108.play: Kneel'd at my feet, and bade me be advised

109.play: Who spake of brotherhood? who spake of love?

110.play: Who told me how the poor soul did forsake

111.play: The mighty Warwick, and did fight for me?

112.play: Who told me, in the field by Tewksbury

113.play: When Oxford had me down, he rescued me,

114.play: And said, 'Dear brother, live, and be a king'?

115.play: Who told me, when we both lay in the field

116.play: Frozen almost to death, how he did lap me

117.play: Even in his own garments, and gave himself,

118.play: All thin and naked, to the numb cold night?

119.play: All this from my remembrance brutish wrath

120.play: Sinfully pluck'd, and not a man of you

121.play: Had so much grace to put it in my mind.

122.play: But when your carters or your waiting-vassals

123.play: Have done a drunken slaughter, and defaced

124.play: The precious image of our dear Redeemer,

125.play: You straight are on your knees for pardon, pardon;

126.play: And I unjustly too, must grant it you

127.play: But for my brother not a man would speak,

128.play: Nor I, ungracious, speak unto myself

129.play: For him, poor soul. The proudest of you all

130.play: Have been beholding to him in his life;

131.play: Yet none of you would once plead for his life.

132.play: O God, I fear thy justice will take hold

133.play: On me, and you, and mine, and yours for this!

134.play: Come, Hastings, help me to my closet.

136.play: GLOUCESTER: This is the fruit of rashness! Mark'd you not

137.play: How that the guilty kindred of the queen

138.play: Look'd pale when they did hear of Clarence' death?

139.play: O, they did urge it still unto the king!

140.play: God will revenge it. But come, let us in,

141.play: To comfort Edward with our company.

142.play: BUCKINGHAM: We wait upon your grace.

1.play: Boy: Tell me, good grandam, is our father dead?

2.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: No, boy.

3.play: Why do you wring your hands, and beat your breast,

4.play: And cry 'O Clarence, my unhappy son!'

5.play: Girl: Why do you look on us, and shake your head,

6.play: And call us wretches, orphans, castaways

7.play: If that our noble father be alive?

8.play: My pretty cousins, you mistake me much;

9.play: I do lament the sickness of the king.

10.play: As loath to lose him, not your father's death;

11.play: It were lost sorrow to wail one that's lost.

12.play: Boy: Then, grandam, you conclude that he is dead.

13.play: The king my uncle is to blame for this:

14.play: God will revenge it; whom I will importune

15.play: With daily prayers all to that effect.

16.play: Girl: And so will I.

17.play: Peace, children, peace! the king doth love you well:

18.play: Incapable and shallow innocents,

19.play: You cannot guess who caused your father's death.

20.play: Boy: Grandam, we can; for my good uncle Gloucester

21.play: Told me, the king, provoked by the queen,

22.play: Devised impeachments to imprison him:

23.play: And when my uncle told me so, he wept,

24.play: And hugg'd me in his arm, and kindly kiss'd my cheek;

25.play: Bade me rely on him as on my father,

26.play: And he would love me dearly as his child.

27.play: Oh, that deceit should steal such gentle shapes,

28.play: And with a virtuous vizard hide foul guile!

29.play: He is my son; yea, and therein my shame;

30.play: Yet from my dugs he drew not this deceit.

31.play: Think you my uncle did dissemble, grandam?

33.play: I cannot think it. Hark! what noise is this?

34.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Oh, who shall hinder me to wail and weep,

35.play: To chide my fortune, and torment myself?

36.play: I'll join with black despair against my soul,

37.play: And to myself become an enemy.

38.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: What means this scene of rude impatience?

39.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: To make an act of tragic violence:

40.play: Edward, my lord, your son, our king, is dead.

41.play: Why grow the branches now the root is wither'd?

42.play: Why wither not the leaves the sap being gone?

43.play: If you will live, lament; if die, be brief,

44.play: That our swift-winged souls may catch the king's;

45.play: Or, like obedient subjects, follow him

46.play: To his new kingdom of perpetual rest.

47.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Ah, so much interest have I in thy sorrow

48.play: As I had title in thy noble husband!

49.play: I have bewept a worthy husband's death,

50.play: And lived by looking on his images:

51.play: But now two mirrors of his princely semblance

52.play: Are crack'd in pieces by malignant death,

53.play: And I for comfort have but one false glass,

54.play: Which grieves me when I see my shame in him.

55.play: Thou art a widow; yet thou art a mother,

56.play: And hast the comfort of thy children left thee:

57.play: But death hath snatch'd my husband from mine arms,

58.play: And pluck'd two crutches from my feeble limbs,

59.play: Edward and Clarence. O, what cause have I,

60.play: Thine being but a moiety of my grief,

61.play: To overgo thy plaints and drown thy cries!

62.play: Good aunt, you wept not for our father's death;

63.play: How can we aid you with our kindred tears?

64.play: Girl: Our fatherless distress was left unmoan'd;

66.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Give me no help in lamentation;

67.play: I am not barren to bring forth complaints

68.play: All springs reduce their currents to mine eyes,

69.play: That I, being govern'd by the watery moon,

70.play: May send forth plenteous tears to drown the world!

71.play: Oh for my husband, for my dear lord Edward!

72.play: Children: Oh for our father, for our dear lord Clarence!

73.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Alas for both, both mine, Edward and Clarence!

74.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: What stay had I but Edward? and he's gone.

75.play: What stay had we but Clarence? and he's gone.

76.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: What stays had I but they? and they are gone.

77.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Was never widow had so dear a loss!

78.play: Were never orphans had so dear a loss!

79.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: Was never mother had so dear a loss!

80.play: Alas, I am the mother of these moans!

81.play: Their woes are parcell'd, mine are general.

82.play: She for an Edward weeps, and so do I;

83.play: I for a Clarence weep, so doth not she:

84.play: These babes for Clarence weep and so do I;

85.play: I for an Edward weep, so do not they:

86.play: Alas, you three, on me, threefold distress'd,

87.play: Pour all your tears! I am your sorrow's nurse,

88.play: And I will pamper it with lamentations.

89.play: DORSET: Comfort, dear mother: God is much displeased

90.play: That you take with unthankfulness, his doing:

91.play: In common worldly things, 'tis call'd ungrateful,

92.play: With dull unwilligness to repay a debt

93.play: Which with a bounteous hand was kindly lent;

94.play: Much more to be thus opposite with heaven,

95.play: For it requires the royal debt it lent you.

96.play: RIVERS: Madam, bethink you, like a careful mother,

97.play: Of the young prince your son: send straight for him

98.play: Let him be crown'd; in him your comfort lives:

99.play: Drown desperate sorrow in dead Edward's grave,

100.play: And plant your joys in living Edward's throne.

101.play: GLOUCESTER: Madam, have comfort: all of us have cause

102.play: To wail the dimming of our shining star;

103.play: But none can cure their harms by wailing them.

104.play: Madam, my mother, I do cry you mercy;

105.play: I did not see your grace: humbly on my knee

107.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: God bless thee; and put meekness in thy mind,

108.play: Love, charity, obedience, and true duty!

109.play: [Aside] Amen; and make me die a good old man!

110.play: That is the butt-end of a mother's blessing:

111.play: I marvel why her grace did leave it out.

112.play: BUCKINGHAM: You cloudy princes and heart-sorrowing peers,

113.play: That bear this mutual heavy load of moan,

114.play: Now cheer each other in each other's love

115.play: Though we have spent our harvest of this king,

116.play: We are to reap the harvest of his son.

117.play: The broken rancour of your high-swoln hearts,

118.play: But lately splinter'd, knit, and join'd together,

119.play: Must gently be preserved, cherish'd, and kept:

120.play: Me seemeth good, that, with some little train,

121.play: Forthwith from Ludlow the young prince be fetch'd

122.play: Hither to London, to be crown'd our king.

123.play: RIVERS: Why with some little train, my Lord of Buckingham?

124.play: BUCKINGHAM: Marry, my lord, lest, by a multitude,

125.play: The new-heal'd wound of malice should break out,

126.play: Which would be so much the more dangerous

127.play: By how much the estate is green and yet ungovern'd:

128.play: Where every horse bears his commanding rein,

129.play: And may direct his course as please himself,

130.play: As well the fear of harm, as harm apparent,

131.play: In my opinion, ought to be prevented.

132.play: GLOUCESTER: I hope the king made peace with all of us

133.play: And the compact is firm and true in me.

134.play: RIVERS: And so in me; and so, I think, in all:

135.play: Yet, since it is but green, it should be put

136.play: To no apparent likelihood of breach,

137.play: Which haply by much company might be urged:

138.play: Therefore I say with noble Buckingham,

139.play: That it is meet so few should fetch the prince.

141.play: GLOUCESTER: Then be it so; and go we to determine

142.play: Who they shall be that straight shall post to Ludlow.

143.play: Madam, and you, my mother, will you go

144.play: To give your censures in this weighty business?

145.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: With all our harts.

146.play: BUCKINGHAM: My lord, whoever journeys to the Prince,

147.play: For God's sake, let not us two be behind;

148.play: For, by the way, I'll sort occasion,

149.play: As index to the story we late talk'd of,

150.play: To part the queen's proud kindred from the king.

151.play: GLOUCESTER: My other self, my counsel's consistory,

152.play: My oracle, my prophet! My dear cousin,

153.play: I, like a child, will go by thy direction.

154.play: Towards Ludlow then, for we'll not stay behind.

1.play: First Citizen: Neighbour, well met: whither away so fast?

2.play: Second Citizen: I promise you, I scarcely know myself:

3.play: Hear you the news abroad?

4.play: First Citizen: Ay, that the king is dead.

5.play: Second Citizen: Bad news, by'r lady; seldom comes the better:

6.play: I fear, I fear 'twill prove a troublous world.

7.play: Third Citizen: Neighbours, God speed!

8.play: First Citizen: Give you good morrow, sir.

9.play: Third Citizen: Doth this news hold of good King Edward's death?

10.play: Second Citizen: Ay, sir, it is too true; God help the while!

11.play: Third Citizen: Then, masters, look to see a troublous world.

12.play: First Citizen: No, no; by God's good grace his son shall reign.

13.play: Third Citizen: Woe to the land that's govern'd by a child!

14.play: Second Citizen: In him there is a hope of government,

15.play: That in his nonage council under him,

16.play: And in his full and ripen'd years himself,

17.play: No doubt, shall then and till then govern well.

18.play: First Citizen: So stood the state when Henry the Sixth

19.play: Was crown'd in Paris but at nine months old.

20.play: Third Citizen: Stood the state so? No, no, good friends, God wot;

21.play: For then this land was famously enrich'd

22.play: With politic grave counsel; then the king

23.play: Had virtuous uncles to protect his grace.

24.play: First Citizen: Why, so hath this, both by the father and mother.

25.play: Third Citizen: Better it were they all came by the father,

26.play: Or by the father there were none at all;

27.play: For emulation now, who shall be nearest,

28.play: Will touch us all too near, if God prevent not.

29.play: O, full of danger is the Duke of Gloucester!

30.play: And the queen's sons and brothers haught and proud:

31.play: And were they to be ruled, and not to rule,

32.play: This sickly land might solace as before.

33.play: First Citizen: Come, come, we fear the worst; all shall be well.

34.play: Third Citizen: When clouds appear, wise men put on their cloaks;

35.play: When great leaves fall, the winter is at hand;

36.play: When the sun sets, who doth not look for night?

37.play: Untimely storms make men expect a dearth.

38.play: All may be well; but, if God sort it so,

39.play: 'Tis more than we deserve, or I expect.

40.play: Second Citizen: Truly, the souls of men are full of dread:

41.play: Ye cannot reason almost with a man

42.play: That looks not heavily and full of fear.

43.play: Third Citizen: Before the times of change, still is it so:

44.play: By a divine instinct men's minds mistrust

45.play: Ensuing dangers; as by proof, we see

46.play: The waters swell before a boisterous storm.

47.play: But leave it all to God. whither away?

48.play: Second Citizen: Marry, we were sent for to the justices.

49.play: Third Citizen: And so was I: I'll bear you company.

1.play: ARCHBISHOP OF YORK: Last night, I hear, they lay at Northampton;

2.play: At Stony-Stratford will they be to-night:

3.play: To-morrow, or next day, they will be here.

4.play: DUCHESS OF YORK: I long with all my heart to see the prince:

5.play: I hope he is much grown since last I saw him.

6.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: But I hear, no; they say my son of York

7.play: Hath almost overta'en him in his growth.

64.play: That do conspire my death with devilish plots

65.play: Of damned witchcraft, and that have prevail'd

66.play: Upon my body with their hellish charms?

67.play: HASTINGS: The tender love I bear your grace, my lord,

68.play: Makes me most forward in this noble presence

69.play: To doom the offenders, whatsoever they be

70.play: I say, my lord, they have deserved death.

71.play: GLOUCESTER: Then be your eyes the witness of this ill:

72.play: See how I am bewitch'd; behold mine arm

73.play: Is, like a blasted sapling, wither'd up:

74.play: And this is Edward's wife, that monstrous witch,

75.play: Consorted with that harlot strumpet Shore,

76.play: That by their witchcraft thus have marked me.

77.play: HASTINGS: If they have done this thing, my gracious lord--

78.play: GLOUCESTER: If I thou protector of this damned strumpet--

79.play: Tellest thou me of 'ifs'? Thou art a traitor:

80.play: Off with his head! Now, by Saint Paul I swear,

81.play: I will not dine until I see the same.

82.play: Lovel and Ratcliff, look that it be done:

83.play: The rest, that love me, rise and follow me.

84.play: HASTINGS: Woe, woe for England! not a whit for me;

85.play: For I, too fond, might have prevented this.

86.play: Stanley did dream the boar did raze his helm;

87.play: But I disdain'd it, and did scorn to fly:

88.play: Three times to-day my foot-cloth horse did stumble,

89.play: And startled, when he look'd upon the Tower,

90.play: As loath to bear me to the slaughter-house.

91.play: O, now I want the priest that spake to me:

92.play: I now repent I told the pursuivant

94.play: How they at Pomfret bloodily were butcher'd,

95.play: And I myself secure in grace and favour.

96.play: O Margaret, Margaret, now thy heavy curse

97.play: Is lighted on poor Hastings' wretched head!

98.play: RATCLIFF: Dispatch, my lord; the duke would be at dinner:

99.play: Make a short shrift; he longs to see your head.

100.play: HASTINGS: O momentary grace of mortal men,

101.play: Which we more hunt for than the grace of God!

102.play: Who builds his hopes in air of your good looks,

103.play: Lives like a drunken sailor on a mast,

104.play: Ready, with every nod, to tumble down

105.play: Into the fatal bowels of the deep.

106.play: LOVEL: Come, come, dispatch; 'tis bootless to exclaim.

107.play: HASTINGS: O bloody Richard! miserable England!

108.play: I prophesy the fearful'st time to thee

109.play: That ever wretched age hath look'd upon.

110.play: Come, lead me to the block; bear him my head.

111.play: They smile at me that shortly shall be dead.

1.play: GLOUCESTER: Come, cousin, canst thou quake, and change thy colour,

2.play: Murder thy breath in the middle of a word,

3.play: And then begin again, and stop again,

4.play: As if thou wert distraught and mad with terror?

5.play: BUCKINGHAM: Tut, I can counterfeit the deep tragedian;

6.play: Speak and look back, and pry on every side,

7.play: Tremble and start at wagging of a straw,

8.play: Intending deep suspicion: ghastly looks

9.play: Are at my service, like enforced smiles;

10.play: And both are ready in their offices,

11.play: At any time, to grace my stratagems.

12.play: But what, is Catesby gone?

13.play: GLOUCESTER: He is; and, see, he brings the mayor along.

14.play: BUCKINGHAM: Lord mayor,--

15.play: GLOUCESTER: Look to the drawbridge there!

16.play: BUCKINGHAM: Hark! a drum.

17.play: GLOUCESTER: Catesby, o'erlook the walls.

18.play: BUCKINGHAM: Lord mayor, the reason we have sent--

19.play: GLOUCESTER: Look back, defend thee, here are enemies.

20.play: BUCKINGHAM: God and our innocency defend and guard us!

21.play: GLOUCESTER: Be patient, they are friends, Ratcliff and Lovel.

22.play: LOVEL: Here is the head of that ignoble traitor,

23.play: The dangerous and unsuspected Hastings.

24.play: GLOUCESTER: So dear I loved the man, that I must weep.

25.play: I took him for the plainest harmless creature

26.play: That breathed upon this earth a Christian;

27.play: Made him my book wherein my soul recorded

28.play: The history of all her secret thoughts:

29.play: So smooth he daub'd his vice with show of virtue,

30.play: That, his apparent open guilt omitted,

31.play: I mean, his conversation with Shore's wife,

32.play: He lived from all attainder of suspect.

33.play: BUCKINGHAM: Well, well, he was the covert'st shelter'd traitor

35.play: Would you imagine, or almost believe,

36.play: Were't not that, by great preservation,

37.play: We live to tell it you, the subtle traitor

38.play: This day had plotted, in the council-house

39.play: To murder me and my good Lord of Gloucester?

40.play: Lord Mayor: What, had he so?

41.play: GLOUCESTER: What, think You we are Turks or infidels?

42.play: Or that we would, against the form of law,

43.play: Proceed thus rashly to the villain's death,

44.play: But that the extreme peril of the case,

45.play: The peace of England and our persons' safety,

47.play: Lord Mayor: Now, fair befall you! he deserved his death;

48.play: And you my good lords, both have well proceeded,

49.play: To warn false traitors from the like attempts.

50.play: I never look'd for better at his hands,

51.play: After he once fell in with Mistress Shore.

52.play: Yet had not we determined he should die,

53.play: Until your lordship came to see his death;

54.play: Which now the loving haste of these our friends,

55.play: Somewhat against our meaning, have prevented:

56.play: Because, my lord, we would have had you heard

57.play: The traitor speak, and timorously confess

58.play: The manner and the purpose of his treason;

59.play: That you might well have signified the same

60.play: Unto the citizens, who haply may

61.play: Misconstrue us in him and wail his death.

62.play: But, my good lord, your grace's word shall serve,

63.play: As well as I had seen and heard him speak

64.play: And doubt you not, right noble princes both,

65.play: But I'll acquaint our duteous citizens

66.play: With all your just proceedings in this cause.

67.play: And to that end we wish'd your lord-ship here,

68.play: To avoid the carping censures of the world.

69.play: BUCKINGHAM: But since you come too late of our intents,

70.play: Yet witness what you hear we did intend:

71.play: And so, my good lord mayor, we bid farewell.

72.play: GLOUCESTER: Go, after, after, cousin Buckingham.

73.play: The mayor towards Guildhall hies him in all post:

74.play: There, at your meet'st advantage of the time,

75.play: Infer the bastardy of Edward's children:

76.play: Tell them how Edward put to death a citizen,

77.play: Only for saying he would make his son

78.play: Heir to the crown; meaning indeed his house,

79.play: Which, by the sign thereof was termed so.

81.play: And bestial appetite in change of lust;

82.play: Which stretched to their servants, daughters, wives,

83.play: Even where his lustful eye or savage heart,

84.play: Without control, listed to make his prey.

85.play: Nay, for a need, thus far come near my person:

86.play: Tell them, when that my mother went with child

87.play: Of that unsatiate Edward, noble York

88.play: My princely father then had wars in France

89.play: And, by just computation of the time,

90.play: Found that the issue was not his begot;

91.play: Which well appeared in his lineaments,

92.play: Being nothing like the noble duke my father:

93.play: But touch this sparingly, as 'twere far off,

94.play: Because you know, my lord, my mother lives.

95.play: BUCKINGHAM: Fear not, my lord, I'll play the orator

96.play: As if the golden fee for which I plead

97.play: Were for myself: and so, my lord, adieu.

98.play: GLOUCESTER: If you thrive well, bring them to Baynard's Castle;

99.play: Where you shall find me well accompanied

100.play: With reverend fathers and well-learned bishops.

101.play: BUCKINGHAM: I go: and towards three or four o'clock

102.play: Look for the news that the Guildhall affords.

103.play: GLOUCESTER: Go, Lovel, with all speed to Doctor Shaw;

104.play: Go thou to Friar Penker; bid them both

105.play: Meet me within this hour at Baynard's Castle.

106.play: Now will I in, to take some privy order,

107.play: To draw the brats of Clarence out of sight;

108.play: And to give notice, that no manner of person

109.play: At any time have recourse unto the princes.

1.play: Scrivener: This is the indictment of the good Lord Hastings;

2.play: Which in a set hand fairly is engross'd,

3.play: That it may be this day read over in Paul's.

4.play: And mark how well the sequel hangs together:

5.play: Eleven hours I spent to write it over,

6.play: For yesternight by Catesby was it brought me;

7.play: The precedent was full as long a-doing:

8.play: And yet within these five hours lived Lord Hastings,

9.play: Untainted, unexamined, free, at liberty

10.play: Here's a good world the while! Why who's so gross,

11.play: That seeth not this palpable device?

12.play: Yet who's so blind, but says he sees it not?

13.play: Bad is the world; and all will come to nought,

14.play: When such bad dealings must be seen in thought.

1.play: GLOUCESTER: How now, my lord, what say the citizens?

2.play: BUCKINGHAM: Now, by the holy mother of our Lord,

3.play: The citizens are mum and speak not a word.

4.play: GLOUCESTER: Touch'd you the bastardy of Edward's children?

5.play: BUCKINGHAM: I did; with his contract with Lady Lucy,

6.play: And his contract by deputy in France;

7.play: The insatiate greediness of his desires,

8.play: And his enforcement of the city wives;

9.play: His tyranny for trifles; his own bastardy,

10.play: As being got, your father then in France,

11.play: His resemblance, being not like the duke;

12.play: Withal I did infer your lineaments,

13.play: Being the right idea of your father,

14.play: Both in your form and nobleness of mind;

15.play: Laid open all your victories in Scotland,

16.play: Your dicipline in war, wisdom in peace,

17.play: Your bounty, virtue, fair humility:

18.play: Indeed, left nothing fitting for the purpose

19.play: Untouch'd, or slightly handled, in discourse

20.play: And when mine oratory grew to an end

21.play: I bid them that did love their country's good

22.play: Cry 'God save Richard, England's royal king!'

23.play: GLOUCESTER: Ah! and did they so?

24.play: BUCKINGHAM: No, so God help me, they spake not a word;

25.play: But, like dumb statues or breathing stones,

26.play: Gazed each on other, and look'd deadly pale.

27.play: Which when I saw, I reprehended them;

28.play: And ask'd the mayor what meant this wilful silence:

29.play: His answer was, the people were not wont

30.play: To be spoke to but by the recorder.

31.play: Then he was urged to tell my tale again,

32.play: 'Thus saith the duke, thus hath the duke inferr'd;'

33.play: But nothing spake in warrant from himself.

34.play: When he had done, some followers of mine own,

35.play: At the lower end of the hall, hurl'd up their caps,

36.play: And some ten voices cried 'God save King Richard!'

37.play: And thus I took the vantage of those few,

38.play: 'Thanks, gentle citizens and friends,' quoth I;

40.play: Argues your wisdoms and your love to Richard: '

41.play: And even here brake off, and came away.

42.play: GLOUCESTER: What tongueless blocks were they! would not they speak?

43.play: BUCKINGHAM: No, by my troth, my lord.

44.play: GLOUCESTER: Will not the mayor then and his brethren come?

45.play: BUCKINGHAM: The mayor is here at hand: intend some fear;

46.play: Be not you spoke with, but by mighty suit:

47.play: And look you get a prayer-book in your hand,

48.play: And stand betwixt two churchmen, good my lord;

49.play: For on that ground I'll build a holy descant:

50.play: And be not easily won to our request:

51.play: Play the maid's part, still answer nay, and take it.

52.play: GLOUCESTER: I go; and if you plead as well for them

53.play: As I can say nay to thee for myself,

54.play: No doubt well bring it to a happy issue.

55.play: BUCKINGHAM: Go, go, up to the leads; the lord mayor knocks.

56.play: Welcome my lord; I dance attendance here;

57.play: I think the duke will not be spoke withal.

58.play: Here comes his servant: how now, Catesby,

60.play: CATESBY: My lord: he doth entreat your grace;

61.play: To visit him to-morrow or next day:

62.play: He is within, with two right reverend fathers,

64.play: And no worldly suit would he be moved,

65.play: To draw him from his holy exercise.

66.play: BUCKINGHAM: Return, good Catesby, to thy lord again;

67.play: Tell him, myself, the mayor and citizens,

68.play: In deep designs and matters of great moment,

69.play: No less importing than our general good,

70.play: Are come to have some conference with his grace.

71.play: CATESBY: I'll tell him what you say, my lord.

72.play: BUCKINGHAM: Ah, ha, my lord, this prince is not an Edward!

73.play: He is not lolling on a lewd day-bed,

74.play: But on his knees at meditation;

75.play: Not dallying with a brace of courtezans,

76.play: But meditating with two deep divines;

77.play: Not sleeping, to engross his idle body,

78.play: But praying, to enrich his watchful soul:

79.play: Happy were England, would this gracious prince

80.play: Take on himself the sovereignty thereof:

81.play: But, sure, I fear, we shall ne'er win him to it.

82.play: Lord Mayor: Marry, God forbid his grace should say us nay!

84.play: How now, Catesby, what says your lord?

86.play: He wonders to what end you have assembled

87.play: Such troops of citizens to speak with him,

88.play: His grace not being warn'd thereof before:

89.play: My lord, he fears you mean no good to him.

90.play: BUCKINGHAM: Sorry I am my noble cousin should

91.play: Suspect me, that I mean no good to him:

92.play: By heaven, I come in perfect love to him;

93.play: And so once more return and tell his grace.

94.play: When holy and devout religious men

95.play: Are at their beads, 'tis hard to draw them thence,

97.play: Lord Mayor: See, where he stands between two clergymen!

98.play: Two props of virtue for a Christian prince,

99.play: To stay him from the fall of vanity:

100.play: And, see, a book of prayer in his hand,

101.play: True ornaments to know a holy man.

103.play: Lend favourable ears to our request;

105.play: Of thy devotion and right Christian zeal.

106.play: GLOUCESTER: My lord, there needs no such apology:

107.play: I rather do beseech you pardon me,

108.play: Who, earnest in the service of my God,

109.play: Neglect the visitation of my friends.

110.play: But, leaving this, what is your grace's pleasure?

111.play: BUCKINGHAM: Even that, I hope, which pleaseth God above,

112.play: And all good men of this ungovern'd isle.

113.play: GLOUCESTER: I do suspect I have done some offence

114.play: That seems disgracious in the city's eyes,

115.play: And that you come to reprehend my ignorance.

116.play: BUCKINGHAM: You have, my lord: would it might please your grace,

117.play: At our entreaties, to amend that fault!

118.play: GLOUCESTER: Else wherefore breathe I in a Christian land?

119.play: BUCKINGHAM: Then know, it is your fault that you resign

120.play: The supreme seat, the throne majestical,

121.play: The scepter'd office of your ancestors,

122.play: Your state of fortune and your due of birth,

123.play: The lineal glory of your royal house,

124.play: To the corruption of a blemished stock:

125.play: Whilst, in the mildness of your sleepy thoughts,

126.play: Which here we waken to our country's good,

127.play: This noble isle doth want her proper limbs;

128.play: Her face defaced with scars of infamy,

129.play: Her royal stock graft with ignoble plants,

130.play: And almost shoulder'd in the swallowing gulf

131.play: Of blind forgetfulness and dark oblivion.

132.play: Which to recure, we heartily solicit

133.play: Your gracious self to take on you the charge

134.play: And kingly government of this your land,

136.play: Or lowly factor for another's gain;

137.play: But as successively from blood to blood,

138.play: Your right of birth, your empery, your own.

139.play: For this, consorted with the citizens,

140.play: Your very worshipful and loving friends,

142.play: In this just suit come I to move your grace.

143.play: GLOUCESTER: I know not whether to depart in silence,

144.play: Or bitterly to speak in your reproof.

145.play: Best fitteth my degree or your condition

146.play: If not to answer, you might haply think

148.play: To bear the golden yoke of sovereignty,

149.play: Which fondly you would here impose on me;

150.play: If to reprove you for this suit of yours,

151.play: So season'd with your faithful love to me.

152.play: Then, on the other side, I cheque'd my friends.

153.play: Therefore, to speak, and to avoid the first,

154.play: And then, in speaking, not to incur the last,

156.play: Your love deserves my thanks; but my desert

158.play: First if all obstacles were cut away,

159.play: And that my path were even to the crown,

160.play: As my ripe revenue and due by birth

161.play: Yet so much is my poverty of spirit,

162.play: So mighty and so many my defects,

163.play: As I had rather hide me from my greatness,

164.play: Being a bark to brook no mighty sea,

165.play: Than in my greatness covet to be hid,

166.play: And in the vapour of my glory smother'd.

167.play: But, God be thank'd, there's no need of me,

168.play: And much I need to help you, if need were;

169.play: The royal tree hath left us royal fruit,

170.play: Which, mellow'd by the stealing hours of time,

171.play: Will well become the seat of majesty,

172.play: And make, no doubt, us happy by his reign.

173.play: On him I lay what you would lay on me,

174.play: The right and fortune of his happy stars;

175.play: Which God defend that I should wring from him!

176.play: BUCKINGHAM: My lord, this argues conscience in your grace;

177.play: But the respects thereof are nice and trivial,

179.play: You say that Edward is your brother's son:

180.play: So say we too, but not by Edward's wife;

181.play: For first he was contract to Lady Lucy--

182.play: Your mother lives a witness to that vow--

184.play: To Bona, sister to the King of France.

185.play: These both put by a poor petitioner,

186.play: A care-crazed mother of a many children,

188.play: Even in the afternoon of her best days,

189.play: Made prize and purchase of his lustful eye,

190.play: Seduced the pitch and height of all his thoughts

191.play: To base declension and loathed bigamy

192.play: By her, in his unlawful bed, he got

193.play: This Edward, whom our manners term the prince.

195.play: Save that, for reverence to some alive,

196.play: I give a sparing limit to my tongue.

197.play: Then, good my lord, take to your royal self

199.play: If non to bless us and the land withal,

200.play: Yet to draw forth your noble ancestry

201.play: From the corruption of abusing times,

203.play: Lord Mayor: Do, good my lord, your citizens entreat you.

204.play: Refuse not, mighty lord, this proffer'd love.

205.play: CATESBY: O, make them joyful, grant their lawful suit!

206.play: GLOUCESTER: Alas, why would you heap these cares on me?

207.play: I am unfit for state and majesty;

208.play: I do beseech you, take it not amiss;

209.play: I cannot nor I will not yield to you.

210.play: BUCKINGHAM: If you refuse it,--as, in love and zeal,

211.play: Loath to depose the child, Your brother's son;

212.play: As well we know your tenderness of heart

214.play: Which we have noted in you to your kin,

215.play: And egally indeed to all estates,--

216.play: Yet whether you accept our suit or no,

217.play: Your brother's son shall never reign our king;

218.play: But we will plant some other in the throne,

219.play: To the disgrace and downfall of your house:

220.play: And in this resolution here we leave you.--

221.play: Come, citizens: 'zounds! I'll entreat no more.

222.play: GLOUCESTER: O, do not swear, my lord of Buckingham.

223.play: CATESBY: Call them again, my lord, and accept their suit.

224.play: ANOTHER: Do, good my lord, lest all the land do rue it.

225.play: GLOUCESTER: Would you enforce me to a world of care?

226.play: Well, call them again. I am not made of stone,

227.play: But penetrable to your. kind entreats,

228.play: Albeit against my conscience and my soul.

229.play: Cousin of Buckingham, and you sage, grave men,

230.play: Since you will buckle fortune on my back,

231.play: To bear her burthen, whether I will or no,

232.play: I must have patience to endure the load:

233.play: But if black scandal or foul-faced reproach

234.play: Attend the sequel of your imposition,

235.play: Your mere enforcement shall acquittance me

236.play: From all the impure blots and stains thereof;

237.play: For God he knows, and you may partly see,

238.play: How far I am from the desire thereof.

239.play: Lord Mayor: God bless your grace! we see it, and will say it.

240.play: In saying so, you shall but say the truth.

241.play: BUCKINGHAM: Then I salute you with this kingly title:

242.play: Long live Richard, England's royal king!

244.play: To-morrow will it please you to be crown'd?

245.play: GLOUCESTER: Even when you please, since you will have it so.

246.play: BUCKINGHAM: To-morrow, then, we will attend your grace:

247.play: And so most joyfully we take our leave.

32.play: And reverend looker on, of two fair queens.

36.play: May have some scope to beat, or else I swoon

40.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: O Dorset, speak not to me, get thee hence!

41.play: Death and destruction dog thee at the heels;

42.play: Thy mother's name is ominous to children.

43.play: If thou wilt outstrip death, go cross the seas,

47.play: And make me die the thrall of Margaret's curse,

50.play: Take all the swift advantage of the hours;

51.play: You shall have letters from me to my son

52.play: To meet you on the way, and welcome you.

53.play: Be not ta'en tardy by unwise delay.

55.play: O my accursed womb, the bed of death!

56.play: A cockatrice hast thou hatch'd to the world,

61.play: Of golden metal that must round my brow

70.play: Which issued from my other angel husband
