105.play: Which so long sunder'd friends should dwell upon:

106.play: God give us leisure for these rites of love!

107.play: Once more, adieu: be valiant, and speed well!

108.play: RICHMOND: Good lords, conduct him to his regiment:

109.play: I'll strive, with troubled thoughts, to take a nap,

110.play: Lest leaden slumber peise me down to-morrow,

111.play: When I should mount with wings of victory:

112.play: Once more, good night, kind lords and gentlemen.

113.play: O Thou, whose captain I account myself,

114.play: Look on my forces with a gracious eye;

115.play: Put in their hands thy bruising irons of wrath,

116.play: That they may crush down with a heavy fall

117.play: The usurping helmets of our adversaries!

118.play: Make us thy ministers of chastisement,

119.play: That we may praise thee in the victory!

120.play: To thee I do commend my watchful soul,

121.play: Ere I let fall the windows of mine eyes:

122.play: Sleeping and waking, O, defend me still!

124.play: of Prince Edward: [To KING RICHARD III]

125.play: Let me sit heavy on thy soul to-morrow!

126.play: Think, how thou stab'dst me in my prime of youth

127.play: At Tewksbury: despair, therefore, and die!

128.play: Be cheerful, Richmond; for the wronged souls

129.play: Of butcher'd princes fight in thy behalf

130.play: King Henry's issue, Richmond, comforts thee.

132.play: of King Henry VI: [To KING RICHARD III]

133.play: When I was mortal, my anointed body

134.play: By thee was punched full of deadly holes

135.play: Think on the Tower and me: despair, and die!

136.play: Harry the Sixth bids thee despair, and die!

137.play: Virtuous and holy, be thou conqueror!

138.play: Harry, that prophesied thou shouldst be king,

139.play: Doth comfort thee in thy sleep: live, and flourish!

140.play: Ghost of CLARENCE: [To KING RICHARD III]

141.play: Let me sit heavy on thy soul to-morrow!

142.play: I, that was wash'd to death with fulsome wine,

143.play: Poor Clarence, by thy guile betrayed to death!

144.play: To-morrow in the battle think on me,

145.play: And fall thy edgeless sword: despair, and die!--

146.play: Thou offspring of the house of Lancaster

147.play: The wronged heirs of York do pray for thee

148.play: Good angels guard thy battle! live, and flourish!

149.play: Ghost of RIVERS: [To KING RICHARD III]

150.play: Let me sit heavy on thy soul to-morrow,

151.play: Rivers. that died at Pomfret! despair, and die!

152.play: Ghost of GREY: [To KING RICHARD III]

153.play: Think upon Grey, and let thy soul despair!

154.play: Ghost of VAUGHAN: [To KING RICHARD III]

155.play: Think upon Vaughan, and, with guilty fear,

156.play: Let fall thy lance: despair, and die!

158.play: Awake, and think our wrongs in Richard's bosom

159.play: Will conquer him! awake, and win the day!

160.play: Ghost of HASTINGS: [To KING RICHARD III]

162.play: And in a bloody battle end thy days!

163.play: Think on Lord Hastings: despair, and die!

165.play: Arm, fight, and conquer, for fair England's sake!

167.play: of young Princes: [To KING RICHARD III]

168.play: Dream on thy cousins smother'd in the Tower:

169.play: Let us be led within thy bosom, Richard,

170.play: And weigh thee down to ruin, shame, and death!

171.play: Thy nephews' souls bid thee despair and die!

172.play: Sleep, Richmond, sleep in peace, and wake in joy;

173.play: Good angels guard thee from the boar's annoy!

174.play: Live, and beget a happy race of kings!

175.play: Edward's unhappy sons do bid thee flourish.

176.play: Ghost of LADY ANNE: [To KING RICHARD III]

177.play: Richard, thy wife, that wretched Anne thy wife,

178.play: That never slept a quiet hour with thee,

179.play: Now fills thy sleep with perturbations

180.play: To-morrow in the battle think on me,

181.play: And fall thy edgeless sword: despair, and die!

182.play: Thou quiet soul, sleep thou a quiet sleep

183.play: Dream of success and happy victory!

184.play: Thy adversary's wife doth pray for thee.

187.play: The last was I that helped thee to the crown;

188.play: The last was I that felt thy tyranny:

189.play: O, in the battle think on Buckingham,

190.play: And die in terror of thy guiltiness!

191.play: Dream on, dream on, of bloody deeds and death:

192.play: Fainting, despair; despairing, yield thy breath!

193.play: I died for hope ere I could lend thee aid:

194.play: But cheer thy heart, and be thou not dismay'd:

195.play: God and good angel fight on Richmond's side;

196.play: And Richard falls in height of all his pride.

197.play: KING RICHARD III: Give me another horse: bind up my wounds.

198.play: Have mercy, Jesu!--Soft! I did but dream.

199.play: O coward conscience, how dost thou afflict me!

200.play: The lights burn blue. It is now dead midnight.

201.play: Cold fearful drops stand on my trembling flesh.

202.play: What do I fear? myself? there's none else by:

203.play: Richard loves Richard; that is, I am I.

204.play: Is there a murderer here? No. Yes, I am:

205.play: Then fly. What, from myself? Great reason why:

206.play: Lest I revenge. What, myself upon myself?

207.play: Alack. I love myself. Wherefore? for any good

208.play: That I myself have done unto myself?

209.play: O, no! alas, I rather hate myself

210.play: For hateful deeds committed by myself!

211.play: I am a villain: yet I lie. I am not.

212.play: Fool, of thyself speak well: fool, do not flatter.

213.play: My conscience hath a thousand several tongues,

214.play: And every tongue brings in a several tale,

215.play: And every tale condemns me for a villain.

216.play: Perjury, perjury, in the high'st degree

217.play: Murder, stem murder, in the direst degree;

218.play: All several sins, all used in each degree,

219.play: Throng to the bar, crying all, Guilty! guilty!

220.play: I shall despair. There is no creature loves me;

221.play: And if I die, no soul shall pity me:

222.play: Nay, wherefore should they, since that I myself

223.play: Find in myself no pity to myself?

224.play: Methought the souls of all that I had murder'd

225.play: Came to my tent; and every one did threat

226.play: To-morrow's vengeance on the head of Richard.

228.play: KING RICHARD III: 'Zounds! who is there?

229.play: Ratcliff, my lord; 'tis I. The early village-cock

230.play: Hath twice done salutation to the morn;

231.play: Your friends are up, and buckle on their armour.

232.play: KING RICHARD III: O Ratcliff, I have dream'd a fearful dream!

233.play: What thinkest thou, will our friends prove all true?

235.play: KING RICHARD III: O Ratcliff, I fear, I fear,--

236.play: Nay, good my lord, be not afraid of shadows.

237.play: KING RICHARD III: By the apostle Paul, shadows to-night

238.play: Have struck more terror to the soul of Richard

239.play: Than can the substance of ten thousand soldiers

240.play: Armed in proof, and led by shallow Richmond.

241.play: It is not yet near day. Come, go with me;

242.play: Under our tents I'll play the eaves-dropper,

243.play: To see if any mean to shrink from me.

245.play: RICHMOND: Cry mercy, lords and watchful gentlemen,

246.play: That you have ta'en a tardy sluggard here.

247.play: LORDS: How have you slept, my lord?

248.play: RICHMOND: The sweetest sleep, and fairest-boding dreams

249.play: That ever enter'd in a drowsy head,

250.play: Have I since your departure had, my lords.

251.play: Methought their souls, whose bodies Richard murder'd,

252.play: Came to my tent, and cried on victory:

253.play: I promise you, my soul is very jocund

254.play: In the remembrance of so fair a dream.

255.play: How far into the morning is it, lords?

256.play: LORDS: Upon the stroke of four.

257.play: RICHMOND: Why, then 'tis time to arm and give direction.

258.play: More than I have said, loving countrymen,

259.play: The leisure and enforcement of the time

260.play: Forbids to dwell upon: yet remember this,

261.play: God and our good cause fight upon our side;

262.play: The prayers of holy saints and wronged souls,

263.play: Like high-rear'd bulwarks, stand before our faces;

264.play: Richard except, those whom we fight against

265.play: Had rather have us win than him they follow:

266.play: For what is he they follow? truly, gentlemen,

267.play: A bloody tyrant and a homicide;

268.play: One raised in blood, and one in blood establish'd;

269.play: One that made means to come by what he hath,

270.play: And slaughter'd those that were the means to help him;

271.play: Abase foul stone, made precious by the foil

272.play: Of England's chair, where he is falsely set;

273.play: One that hath ever been God's enemy:

274.play: Then, if you fight against God's enemy,

275.play: God will in justice ward you as his soldiers;

276.play: If you do sweat to put a tyrant down,

277.play: You sleep in peace, the tyrant being slain;

278.play: If you do fight against your country's foes,

279.play: Your country's fat shall pay your pains the hire;

280.play: If you do fight in safeguard of your wives,

281.play: Your wives shall welcome home the conquerors;

282.play: If you do free your children from the sword,

283.play: Your children's children quit it in your age.

284.play: Then, in the name of God and all these rights,

285.play: Advance your standards, draw your willing swords.

286.play: For me, the ransom of my bold attempt

287.play: Shall be this cold corpse on the earth's cold face;

288.play: But if I thrive, the gain of my attempt

289.play: The least of you shall share his part thereof.

290.play: Sound drums and trumpets boldly and cheerfully;

291.play: God and Saint George! Richmond and victory!

292.play: KING RICHARD III: What said Northumberland as touching Richmond?

293.play: RATCLIFF: That he was never trained up in arms.

294.play: KING RICHARD III: He said the truth: and what said Surrey then?

295.play: He smiled and said 'The better for our purpose.'

296.play: KING RICHARD III: He was in the right; and so indeed it is.

297.play: Ten the clock there. Give me a calendar.

300.play: KING RICHARD III: Then he disdains to shine; for by the book

301.play: He should have braved the east an hour ago

302.play: A black day will it be to somebody. Ratcliff!

304.play: KING RICHARD III: The sun will not be seen to-day;

305.play: The sky doth frown and lour upon our army.

306.play: I would these dewy tears were from the ground.

307.play: Not shine to-day! Why, what is that to me

308.play: More than to Richmond? for the selfsame heaven

309.play: That frowns on me looks sadly upon him.

310.play: NORFOLK: Arm, arm, my lord; the foe vaunts in the field.

311.play: KING RICHARD III: Come, bustle, bustle; caparison my horse.

312.play: Call up Lord Stanley, bid him bring his power:

313.play: I will lead forth my soldiers to the plain,

314.play: And thus my battle shall be ordered:

315.play: My foreward shall be drawn out all in length,

316.play: Consisting equally of horse and foot;

317.play: Our archers shall be placed in the midst

318.play: John Duke of Norfolk, Thomas Earl of Surrey,

319.play: Shall have the leading of this foot and horse.

320.play: They thus directed, we will follow

321.play: In the main battle, whose puissance on either side

322.play: Shall be well winged with our chiefest horse.

323.play: This, and Saint George to boot! What think'st thou, Norfolk?

325.play: This found I on my tent this morning.

327.play: 'Jockey of Norfolk, be not too bold,

328.play: For Dickon thy master is bought and sold.'

329.play: A thing devised by the enemy.

330.play: Go, gentleman, every man unto his charge

331.play: Let not our babbling dreams affright our souls:

332.play: Conscience is but a word that cowards use,

333.play: Devised at first to keep the strong in awe:

334.play: Our strong arms be our conscience, swords our law.

335.play: March on, join bravely, let us to't pell-mell

336.play: If not to heaven, then hand in hand to hell.

337.play: What shall I say more than I have inferr'd?

338.play: Remember whom you are to cope withal;

339.play: A sort of vagabonds, rascals, and runaways,

340.play: A scum of Bretons, and base lackey peasants,

341.play: Whom their o'er-cloyed country vomits forth

342.play: To desperate ventures and assured destruction.

343.play: You sleeping safe, they bring to you unrest;

344.play: You having lands, and blest with beauteous wives,

345.play: They would restrain the one, distain the other.

346.play: And who doth lead them but a paltry fellow,

347.play: Long kept in Bretagne at our mother's cost?

348.play: A milk-sop, one that never in his life

349.play: Felt so much cold as over shoes in snow?

350.play: Let's whip these stragglers o'er the seas again;

351.play: Lash hence these overweening rags of France,

352.play: These famish'd beggars, weary of their lives;

353.play: Who, but for dreaming on this fond exploit,

354.play: For want of means, poor rats, had hang'd themselves:

355.play: If we be conquer'd, let men conquer us,

356.play: And not these bastard Bretons; whom our fathers

357.play: Have in their own land beaten, bobb'd, and thump'd,

358.play: And in record, left them the heirs of shame.

359.play: Shall these enjoy our lands? lie with our wives?

362.play: Fight, gentlemen of England! fight, bold yoemen!

363.play: Draw, archers, draw your arrows to the head!

364.play: Spur your proud horses hard, and ride in blood;

365.play: Amaze the welkin with your broken staves!

366.play: What says Lord Stanley? will he bring his power?

367.play: Messenger: My lord, he doth deny to come.

368.play: Off with his son George's head!

369.play: NORFOLK: My lord, the enemy is past the marsh

370.play: After the battle let George Stanley die.

371.play: KING RICHARD III: A thousand hearts are great within my bosom:

372.play: Advance our standards, set upon our foes

373.play: Our ancient word of courage, fair Saint George,

374.play: Inspire us with the spleen of fiery dragons!

375.play: Upon them! victory sits on our helms.

1.play: CATESBY: Rescue, my Lord of Norfolk, rescue, rescue!

2.play: The king enacts more wonders than a man,

3.play: Daring an opposite to every danger:

4.play: His horse is slain, and all on foot he fights,

5.play: Seeking for Richmond in the throat of death.

6.play: Rescue, fair lord, or else the day is lost!

7.play: KING RICHARD III: A horse! a horse! my kingdom for a horse!

8.play: Withdraw, my lord; I'll help you to a horse.

9.play: KING RICHARD III: Slave, I have set my life upon a cast,

10.play: And I will stand the hazard of the die:

11.play: I think there be six Richmonds in the field;

12.play: Five have I slain to-day instead of him.

13.play: A horse! a horse! my kingdom for a horse!

1.play: RICHMOND: God and your arms be praised, victorious friends,

2.play: The day is ours, the bloody dog is dead.

3.play: DERBY: Courageous Richmond, well hast thou acquit thee.

4.play: Lo, here, this long-usurped royalty

5.play: From the dead temples of this bloody wretch

6.play: Have I pluck'd off, to grace thy brows withal:

7.play: Wear it, enjoy it, and make much of it.

8.play: RICHMOND: Great God of heaven, say Amen to all!

9.play: But, tell me, is young George Stanley living?

10.play: DERBY: He is, my lord, and safe in Leicester town;

11.play: Whither, if it please you, we may now withdraw us.

12.play: RICHMOND: What men of name are slain on either side?

13.play: DERBY: John Duke of Norfolk, Walter Lord Ferrers,

14.play: Sir Robert Brakenbury, and Sir William Brandon.
